source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That the ‘Log_connections’ Database Flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL Instance Is Set to ‘On’

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That the ‘Log_connections’ Database Flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL Instance Is Set to ‘On’
input_row.description: Enabling the log_connections setting causes each attempted connection to the server to be logged, along with successful completion of client authentication. This parameter cannot be changed after the session starts.
input_row.rationale: PostgreSQL does not log attempted connections by default. Enabling the log_connections setting will create log entries for each attempted connection as well as successful completion of client authentication which can be useful in troubleshooting issues and to determine any unusual connection attempts to the server. This recommendation is applicable to PostgreSQL database instances. Impact: Turning on logging will increase the required storage over time. Mismanaged logs may cause your storage costs to increase. Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview page. 3. Go to the Configuration card. 4. Under Database flags, check the value of log_connections flag to determine if it is configured as expected. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Ensure the below command returns on for every Cloud SQL PostgreSQL database instance: gcloud sql instances describe [INSTANCE_NAME] --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="log_connections")|.value' In the output, database flags are listed under the settings as the collection databaseFlags.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the PostgreSQL instance for which you want to enable the database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add a Database Flag, choose the flag log_connections from the drop-down menu and set the value as on. 6. Click Save. 7. Confirm the changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the log_connections database flag for every Cloud SQL PosgreSQL database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch <INSTANCE_NAME> --database-flags "log_connections"=on Note : This command will overwrite all previously set database flags. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags to be set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("="). Default Value: By default log_connections is off. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags 2. https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config-logging.html#GUC-LOG- CONNECTIONS Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note : some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see the Operational Guidelines. Note : Configuring the above flag does not require restarting the Cloud SQL instance.
input_row.impact: Turning on logging will increase the required storage over time. Mismanaged logs may cause your storage costs to increase. Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags 2. https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config-logging.html#GUC-LOG- CONNECTIONS Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note : some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see the Operational Guidelines. Note : Configuring the above flag does not require restarting the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI. The audit instructions provide a specific Google Cloud CLI command to check the `log_connections` flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances, confirming that this can be done programmatically.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_log_connections_flag_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) to interact with Cloud SQL instances.
- Execute the command `gcloud sql instances describe [INSTANCE_NAME] --format=json` to retrieve instance settings.
- Parse the output and check for the presence of `databaseFlags` with the name `log_connections`.
- Verify that the value of `log_connections` is set to `'on'`.
- Apply this check to each Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance within the GCP project.
- Pass Criteria: The `log_connections` flag must be explicitly set to `on` for compliance.
- This approach should be executed across all regions and applicable PostgreSQL instances to ensure comprehensive coverage.
- References used include the CIS benchmark documentation and Google's documentation on Cloud SQL flags.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. The control can be fully verified programmatically using GCP APIs or the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud` CLI). There is no need for manual steps if all Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances are queried via API/CLI.

- **GCP SDK/CLI Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud sql instances describe [INSTANCE_NAME]` command retrieves all instance metadata, including `databaseFlags`. This information is exposed via both the `gcloud` CLI and the Cloud SQL Admin API (`instances.get` method).

- **Other Considerations:**  
  GCP Config (now called "Config Connector") does not natively support compliance checks for database flags at this granularity, so the CLI/API approach is preferred.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The described approach is technically correct:  
    - Use `gcloud sql instances list` to enumerate all instances.
    - Use `gcloud sql instances describe [INSTANCE_NAME]` to fetch the configuration.
    - Parse `settings.databaseFlags` for a flag named `log_connections` with value `'on'`.
  - The check should be performed for all PostgreSQL instances (not MySQL/SQL Server).
  - The command and parsing logic are valid.

- **Program Name:**  
  - The name `gcp_sql_instance_log_connections_flag_enabled` is descriptive but could be more precise (see Improvements).

- **Minor Omissions:**  
  - The approach does not explicitly mention filtering for PostgreSQL instances only.
  - It does not handle the case where the flag is unset (default is `'off'`).
  - It does not specify how to handle instances with no `databaseFlags` set (should be non-compliant).

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Filter for PostgreSQL:**  
  - Ensure only PostgreSQL instances are checked. Use `gcloud sql instances list --filter="databaseVersion~^POSTGRES"` or parse the `databaseVersion` field in the describe output.

- **Handle Unset Flags:**  
  - If `log_connections` is not set, the default is `'off'` (non-compliant). The check should explicitly fail in this case.

- **API Alternative:**  
  - The Cloud SQL Admin API (`projects.instances.list` and `projects.instances.get`) can be used for automation at scale, especially for organizations with many projects.

- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - For clarity and convention, use:  
    `gcp_sql_postgresql_instance_log_connections_flag_on`  
    This makes it clear it's only for PostgreSQL, and the expected value is `'on'`.

- **Comprehensive Coverage:**  
  - Ensure the automation iterates over all projects if multi-project environments are in scope.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is technically sound and aligns with GCP security best practices and available tooling.
- Minor improvements are noted, but the core approach is valid.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **MODIFY**

- **Modify the automation to:**
  - Explicitly filter for PostgreSQL instances.
  - Fail the check if `log_connections` is not set (since default is `'off'`).
  - Consider renaming the program to `gcp_sql_postgresql_instance_log_connections_flag_on` for clarity and convention.

- **No need to reclassify** (remains Automated).

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment           |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE               |
| Technical Accuracy   | 8/10                |
| Improvements         | See above           |
| Confidence           | HIGH                |
| Final Recommendation | MODIFY              |

---
